themes: Deep Learning Education & Building from Scratch
AI as an Extension of Computing
Autonomous Driving & The March of Nines

principles.label: Build from Scratch to Understand
principles.summary: To truly understand deep learning, you must manually build neural networks and their core algorithms (like backpropagation) from scratch without relying on automated tools or copy-pasting.
principles.details: Manually implementing components builds a strong intuitive understanding of how gradients flow and how neural nets are optimized. There are micro-details and gaps in knowledge that only reveal themselves when you are forced to arrange the components yourself.
principles.representative_quote: If I can't build it, I don't understand it... I 100% have always believed this very strongly, because there are all these micro things that are just not properly arranged and you don't really have the knowledge.

principles.label: Build Digital Infrastructure for Agents
principles.summary: AI agents represent a fundamentally new category of digital consumers, requiring machine-readable infrastructure rather than human-centric GUIs.
principles.details: Parsing human-centric HTML or instructions like 'click here' is error-prone for LLMs. Toolmakers must design interfaces and documentation (like markdown or llms.txt) that are easily parseable by AI to enable agentic workflows.
principles.representative_quote: there is new category of consumer/manipulator of digital information

principles.label: Continuous Diffusion of AGI
principles.summary: AGI will not be a sudden, discrete event that breaks economic trends, but a gradual diffusion of automation.
principles.details: Historically, transformative technologies like computers and the internet diffused gradually. AGI will likely follow the same exponential growth curve rather than causing a sudden vertical spike in GDP.

principles.label: English as the Ultimate Interface
principles.summary: Natural language is replacing traditional syntax as the primary programming language and label space.
principles.details: Whether prompting advanced LLMs to write code or using language as a rich, flexible label space for computer vision, natural language enables significantly easier and more expressive human-computer interaction.
principles.representative_quote: The hottest new programming language is English

principles.label: Keep the AI on a Leash
principles.summary: LLMs are fallible and can easily get lost; humans must remain in the loop to verify their work in small, concrete chunks.
principles.details: Instead of asking an AI for a massive 10,000-line code diff, work incrementally. Scrutinize LLM-generated code like you would a very junior, absent-minded data analyst who might make sneaky implicit assumptions.
principles.representative_quote: we have to keep the AI on the leash. We I think a lot of people are getting way over excited with AI agents and uh it's not useful to me to get a diff of 10,000 lines of code to my repo.

principles.label: Software 3.0 is Eating 1.0 and 2.0
principles.summary: Programming is shifting from writing explicit code (1.0) and training neural network weights (2.0) to prompting Large Language Models in natural language (3.0).
principles.details: Prompts are becoming the new programs, which will lead to a massive rewrite of existing software stacks. Software engineers should strive to be fluent in all three paradigms, transitioning fluidly between them depending on the functionality required.
principles.representative_quote: Software 3.0 is eating 1.0/2.0

principles.label: Agency Over Intelligence
principles.summary: In an era where AI commoditizes intelligence, human agency becomes the most powerful and scarce resource.
principles.details: Society has historically venerated IQ and raw intelligence, but the ability to take action, direct systems, and drive outcomes is now the true differentiator.

principles.label: AI Exposure vs. Replacement
principles.summary: High AI exposure indicates a job will be reshaped, not necessarily replaced.
principles.details: Increased productivity from AI could actually grow demand for a profession. Raw exposure scores don't account for demand elasticity, latent demand, or regulatory barriers.
principles.representative_quote: Many high-exposure jobs will be reshaped, not replaced.

principles.label: Base Models are Internet Document Simulators
principles.summary: A base model is trained purely to predict the next token on internet text. It acts as a glorified autocomplete and is not an assistant.
principles.details: Base models will not answer questions directly unless prompted cleverly to look like a web page, because they lack the conversational formatting of an assistant.
principles.representative_quote: the base models are not very often released because they're kind of just only a step one of a few other steps that we still need to take to get in system

principles.label: Build to Understand
principles.summary: True understanding of a complex system only comes from building it from scratch.
principles.details: Writing blog posts or slides is insufficient. You must arrange the code and get it to work to confront the micro-details and gaps in your knowledge.

principles.label: Code is Ephemeral and Discardable
principles.summary: Because AI has crossed a capability threshold where programs can be built simply via English, the cost of writing code has plummeted.
principles.details: This allows developers to write single-use, throwaway software, fundamentally altering job descriptions and terraforming the software industry.
principles.representative_quote: code is suddenly free, ephemeral, malleable, discardable after single use.

principles.label: Education as Building Ramps
principles.summary: Effective education is the technical process of untangling complex knowledge and building accessible ramps for students.
principles.details: Learning fails when students bounce off material that is too easy or too hard. Teachers must identify the first-order component, present the pain before the solution, and ensure students are always appropriately challenged.
principles.representative_quote: In my mind, education is the very difficult technical process of building ramps to knowledge.

principles.label: End-to-End Optimization
principles.summary: Train models end-to-end rather than building complex, multi-stage processing pipelines.
principles.details: End-to-end training allows the model to automatically discover intermediate representations and formulate the problem as a single optimization task where all components share the same objective.
principles.representative_quote: Instead of designing and staging explicit algorithms to process images and sentences in complex processing pipelines, our contribution lies in the design of hybrid convolutional and recurrent neural network architectures that connect visual data and natural language utterances with a single network.

principles.label: Human Cognition ≠ LLM Cognition
principles.summary: What is easy for a human is often hard for an LLM, and vice versa. Humans cannot perfectly hardcode optimal reasoning traces for models because the two systems process information fundamentally differently.
principles.details: For example, humans easily count letters or make intuitive leaps, which LLMs struggle with due to tokenization. Conversely, LLMs can instantly access vast, PhD-level knowledge across multiple domains.
principles.representative_quote: what is easy for you or I as or as human labelers what's easy for us or hard for us is different than what's easy or hard for the llm it cognition is different

principles.label: Implicit Programming by Example
principles.summary: LLM assistants are programmed implicitly by providing examples of ideal behavior, rather than with explicit code.
principles.details: Because neural networks are massive mathematical expressions, we cannot write explicit logic for how an assistant should behave. We create datasets of ideal conversations and have the model learn the statistical patterns.
principles.representative_quote: we are going to be implicitly programming the assistant by creating data sets of conversations

principles.label: Lidar and HD Maps are Unscalable
principles.summary: Collecting, building, and maintaining high-definition lidar maps globally is incredibly expensive and difficult to keep up to date.
principles.details: A general vision system that interprets the scene for the first time is harder to build initially but infinitely more scalable in the long run.
principles.representative_quote: it's actually quite unscalable to uh to actually collect build and maintain these high definition lidar maps

principles.label: LLM Apps Animate General Models into Professionals
principles.summary: While foundation labs produce generally capable models, the application layer is required to turn them into deployed professionals.
principles.details: LLM apps organize, finetune, and provide the private data, context engineering, and feedback loops needed for specific verticals.
principles.representative_quote: Personally I suspect that LLM labs will trend to graduate the generally capable college student, but LLM apps will organize, finetune and actually animate teams of them into deployed professionals in specific verticals

principles.label: LLMs are Statistical Simulators, Not Magical AIs
principles.summary: Language models are not sentient entities or biological brains; they are stateless mathematical functions that statistically simulate the human labelers who created their training data.
principles.details: When you prompt an LLM, it doesn't use infinite intelligence to find an answer. It generates text that statistically aligns with its training set, acting as an instantaneous, lossy simulation of an expert human contractor.

principles.label: Never Read Books Alone
principles.summary: Reading alongside an LLM dramatically increases retention, understanding, and the courage to approach dense or out-of-domain texts.
principles.details: Load the raw text of a document or book chapter into the LLM, ask for a summary, and then read the text slowly, asking the LLM questions back and forth whenever you get confused.
principles.representative_quote: I find that basically going hand inhand with llms uh dramatically creases my retention my understanding of these chapters... don't read books alone

principles.label: Poor Memorization as a Feature
principles.summary: Human inability to perfectly memorize data forces the extraction of generalizable patterns.
principles.details: Unlike LLMs, which can get distracted by their massive memorized pre-training data, humans must find general patterns because their rote memorization is poor.
principles.representative_quote: Because we're not that good at memorization, we're forced to find patterns in a more general sense.

principles.label: Pre-training as Crappy Evolution
principles.summary: Pre-training on internet data is a practical substitute for biological evolution.
principles.details: We don't know how to replicate biological evolution in code, but imitating human-generated internet documents works to boot up knowledge and intelligence, providing a starting point.
principles.representative_quote: That's why I call pre-training this crappy evolution. It's the practically possible version with our technology and what we have available to us to get to a starting point.

principles.label: Reinforcement Learning Unlocks Emergent Reasoning
principles.summary: While imitating experts (Supervised Fine-Tuning) caps performance at human levels, Reinforcement Learning allows models to discover novel cognitive strategies and surpass human performance.
principles.details: By optimizing for the correct answer through trial and error in verifiable domains, models naturally learn emergent behaviors like backtracking, reframing, and using chains of thought.
principles.representative_quote: it's rediscovering what happens in your head not what you put down on the solution and there is no human who can hardcode this stuff in the ideal assistant response

principles.label: RLHF is Gameable and Limited
principles.summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is not 'real' RL because it relies on a neural network simulator of human preferences, which is inherently gameable.
principles.details: If you run RL against a reward model for too long, the optimization process will inevitably find adversarial examples that trick the simulator into giving high scores to nonsensical outputs.
principles.representative_quote: I kind of see rhf as not real RL because the reward function is gameable so it's kind of more like in the realm of like little fine-tuning

principles.label: Separate Algorithm from Efficiency
principles.summary: When learning or building foundational AI, separate the core mathematical algorithm from the surrounding engineering optimizations.
principles.details: By stripping away dependencies and efficiency engineering, you can isolate the fundamental algorithm of a neural network (often fitting in a single file), making it vastly easier to understand.

principles.label: Software Engineering as the Foundation
principles.summary: Strong software engineering is the primary prerequisite for AI and deep learning roles.
principles.details: Deep learning specifics can be taught on the job, but a solid foundation in software engineering is essential for building, scaling, and deploying models effectively.
principles.representative_quote: Strong software engineering is the primary requirement. Except for the scientist role, deep learning interest or knowledge is only a bonus (we will teach you).

principles.label: The Human Bottleneck
principles.summary: The human operator is now the ultimate binding constraint on maximum system intelligence and token throughput.
principles.details: Engineers are no longer constrained by compute power. To maximize leverage, humans must step outside the execution loop and act as directors who set boundary conditions for autonomous agents.
principles.representative_quote: The human operator is now the ultimate binding constraint on maximum system intelligence and token throughput.

principles.label: Tokenization is a Flawed Paradigm
principles.summary: Tokenization is a completely separate pipeline stage that introduces a host of weird behaviors and fundamental problems into Large Language Models.
principles.details: Because models see chunks of text rather than individual characters, they struggle with spelling, counting, and string manipulation. Ideally, this stage should be eliminated entirely in future architectures.
principles.representative_quote: In the process, we will see that a lot of weird behaviors and problems of LLMs actually trace back to tokenization.

principles.label: Tokens are Compute
principles.summary: A neural network has a finite, roughly fixed amount of computation it can perform per token. To solve complex problems, reasoning must be distributed across many tokens.
principles.details: Asking a model to solve a complex problem in a single token will fail because it cannot squeeze arbitrary computation into one forward pass. Generating intermediate results allows the model to 'think'.
principles.representative_quote: we actually have to distribute our reasoning and our computation across many tokens because every single token is only spending a finite amount of computation on it

principles.label: Treat the Context Window as a Precious Resource
principles.summary: The context window is the LLM's short-term working memory; overloading it degrades performance.
principles.details: Leaving irrelevant tokens in the context window distracts the model, decreases its accuracy, and makes generating the next token slower and slightly more expensive. Always start a new chat when switching topics.

principles.label: Vertical Integration Accelerates AI
principles.summary: Owning the entire stack allows a team to co-design and engineer without being held back by third parties.
principles.details: In autonomous driving, this means owning the vehicle, sensors, data sourcing, annotation, training cluster, and custom silicon.
principles.representative_quote: everything is very vertical integrated in the team and i think that's pretty incredible because um you get to really co-design an engineer at all the layers of that stack

principles.label: Vision Alone is Capable of Depth Sensing
principles.summary: With a massive, clean, and diverse dataset, synthetic neural networks can match or exceed radar's depth and velocity estimation using only cameras.
principles.details: Humans drive using vision, proving it contains the necessary information. Adding inferior sensors like radar often acts as a crutch that injects noise rather than improving the system.
principles.representative_quote: vision alone is actually in our is our finding that is perfectly capable of depth sensing

frameworks.label: The Autonomy Slider
frameworks.summary: A framework for evaluating the impact of AI tools on programming and knowledge work by progressively raising the layer of abstraction.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Provide a traditional interface for manual work. 2. Integrate LLMs to handle larger chunks of context. 3. Build an application-specific GUI to allow humans to easily audit the AI's work. 4. Provide varying levels of autonomy (e.g., tab completion vs. full repo rewrite) that the user can tune based on task complexity.

frameworks.label: Vibe Coding
frameworks.summary: A new paradigm of software development where humans direct autonomous agents via natural language.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Use a dedicated AI code editor (like Cursor) with access to your local file system. 2. Use the 'composer' feature. 3. Give high-level natural language commands. 4. Sit back and let the agent edit across multiple files autonomously, verifying the results.
frameworks.representative_quote: Vibe coding just refers to letting um giving in giving the control to composer and just telling it what to do

frameworks.label: Auto Research / The Claw
frameworks.summary: An asynchronous loop where AI agents operate autonomously to optimize codebases or architectures.
frameworks.details: Remove the human completely from the experimental loop. Set initial constraints and boundary conditions. Allow the AI to propose an idea, write the code, test it against an objective metric, and iterate entirely on its own.
frameworks.representative_quote: It is an environment where an AI proposes an idea, writes the code, tests it, and iterates entirely on its own.

frameworks.label: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) Tokenization
frameworks.summary: The algorithm for converting raw text into a 1D sequence of symbols for a neural network, balancing sequence length and vocabulary size.
frameworks.details: Start with raw bytes. Find consecutive bytes/symbols that occur very frequently together. Group the pair into a single new symbol (minting a new ID). Rewrite the sequence using the new symbol, decreasing sequence length while increasing vocabulary size. Iterate until reaching the target vocabulary size.

frameworks.label: Empirical Factuality Tuning
frameworks.summary: A pipeline for reducing hallucinations by teaching an LLM to say 'I don't know' when it lacks internal knowledge.
frameworks.details: Generate factual questions from a training document. Interrogate the target model multiple times. Use an LLM judge to compare answers. If the model consistently fails, create a new conversation in the training set where the correct target answer is 'I'm sorry, I don't know'.

frameworks.label: Few-Shot Prompt Construction
frameworks.summary: A structured approach to prompting that consistently increases the accuracy of language models.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Provide background information and context. 2. Describe the task clearly in words. 3. Provide concrete examples of the input and the desired output. 4. Use XML-like tags to clearly delineate where examples begin and end.

frameworks.label: Learning by Building (Nanochat Approach)
frameworks.summary: A method for deeply understanding complex systems by recreating them from scratch without copy-pasting.
frameworks.details: Put the reference code on a separate monitor. Build the project from the start, growing it in chunks. Allow yourself to reference the original code, but strictly forbid copy-pasting to force yourself to come to terms with micro-details.
frameworks.representative_quote: You want to build it from scratch, you build it from the start. You're not allowed to copy-paste, you're allowed to reference, you're not allowed to copy-paste.

frameworks.label: LLM Pre-training Data Pipeline
frameworks.summary: The process of preparing a massive, high-quality dataset from the internet to train a foundational language model.
frameworks.details: Steps include: Scraping raw HTML (e.g., Common Crawl), applying URL filtering to remove spam/malware, extracting raw text by stripping HTML/CSS, applying language filtering, and finally deduplicating the text and removing Personally Identifiable Information (PII).

frameworks.label: Recommendation Sorting Formula
frameworks.summary: A mathematical approach to curating lists that highlights hidden gems.
frameworks.details: Evaluate how highly you recommend the item. Evaluate how obscure the item is. Multiply the recommendation score by the obscurity score. Sort the final list in descending order based on this product.
frameworks.representative_quote: sorted by the product of (recommended * obscure), descending.

frameworks.label: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
frameworks.summary: A method for training models in unverifiable domains (like creative writing) where candidate solutions cannot be easily scored against a concrete correct answer.
frameworks.details: Generate multiple rollouts from a single prompt. Ask a human to order them from best to worst. Train a separate neural network (the reward model) to output scores consistent with the human ordering. Run reinforcement learning against this reward model.
frameworks.representative_quote: we basically train simulators of humans and RL with respect to those simulators

frameworks.label: The Data Engine
frameworks.summary: An iterative loop for improving a neural network's performance on rare edge cases and difficult real-world scenarios.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Train a neural network on a seed dataset. 2. Deploy silently in 'shadow mode'. 3. Use triggers to source disagreements between the network and legacy systems/drivers. 4. Send flagged clips to an offline auto-labeling pipeline. 5. Incorporate newly labeled hard examples into the training set. 6. Repeat.
frameworks.representative_quote: we spin this loop over and over again until the network basically becomes incredibly good

frameworks.label: The LLM Council
frameworks.summary: A method for solving difficult problems or seeking ideation by consulting multiple top-tier models.
frameworks.details: Maintain access to multiple top-tier LLMs from different providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI). Ask all of the models the exact same question and compare their responses to find the best solution or consensus.

frameworks.label: The March of Nines
frameworks.summary: A framework for evaluating the progress of safety-critical AI systems from demo to deployed product.
frameworks.details: Recognize that a successful demo (working 90% of the time) is only the first 'nine'. Achieving each subsequent nine of reliability (99%, 99.9%, 99.99%) requires a constant, massive amount of additional work to patch edge cases.
frameworks.representative_quote: What takes the long amount of time and the way to think about it is that it's a march of nines. Every single nine is a constant amount of work.

frameworks.label: The Three Stages of LLM Training
frameworks.summary: The standard pipeline for transforming raw internet text into a helpful conversational AI assistant.
frameworks.details: 1. Pre-training: Train on internet documents to build a base model (an internet document simulator) that acts as a knowledge base. 2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Train on human-curated conversations to create an assistant that imitates expert behavior and formatting. 3. Reinforcement Learning (RL): Allow the model to practice generating many solutions, checking answers, and reinforcing the token sequences that reliably lead to correct results.

frameworks.label: Untangling Knowledge
frameworks.summary: A pedagogical framework for designing educational material by isolating first-order components.
frameworks.details: Identify the core essence of the system. Create the simplest possible implementation (e.g., a 100-line script). Present the pain/problem before the solution. Prompt the student to guess the solution. Tack on second-order terms (efficiency, scaling) only after the core is understood.
frameworks.representative_quote: I'm trying to find, what is the thing that matters? What is the first-order component? How can I simplify it? How can I have a simplest thing that shows that thing, shows it in action, and then I can tack on the other terms?

mental_models.label: Jagged Intelligence
mental_models.summary: The unintuitive reality that state-of-the-art LLMs can perform highly complex tasks while simultaneously failing at very simple, dumb problems.
mental_models.details: Because LLMs spike in capability around verifiable domains (like math or code), they can simultaneously act as a genius polymath and a confused grade schooler. Their capabilities do not correlate linearly like human intelligence.
mental_models.representative_quote: Some things work extremely well (by human standards) while some things fail catastrophically (again by human standards), and it's not always obvious which is which

mental_models.label: Weights as 'Hazy Recollection' vs. Context Window as 'Working Memory'
mental_models.summary: Information stored in a neural network's weights is highly compressed and hazily recalled, whereas information placed in the context window is directly and immediately accessible.
mental_models.details: Asking an LLM about a book from its training data yields roughly correct but hazy answers (long-term memory). Pasting a full chapter into the context window yields precise, accurate answers (working memory).
mental_models.representative_quote: knowledge in the parameters of the neural network is a vague recollection the knowledge in the tokens that make up the context window is the working memory

mental_models.label: Anterograde Amnesia / People Spirits
mental_models.summary: Treating LLMs as stochastic simulations of humans that possess encyclopedic memory but suffer from severe cognitive deficits, like the inability to form new long-term memories.
mental_models.details: LLMs are like a coworker who cannot consolidate long-running expertise after training. They rely entirely on their short-term memory (the context window), much like the protagonists in Memento or 50 First Dates.
mental_models.representative_quote: LLMs are a bit like a coworker with Anterograde amnesia - they don't consolidate or build long-running knowledge or expertise once training is over and all they have is short-term memory (context window).

mental_models.label: LLMs as Operating Systems
mental_models.summary: Viewing the LLM not just as a chatbot, but as a new kind of computer where the LLM is the CPU and the context window is the RAM.
mental_models.details: In this model, the LLM orchestrates tools and compute. Currently, it resembles the 1960s era of time-shared mainframe computing, but it is rapidly evolving into the foundational OS for Software 3.0.

mental_models.label: The Iron Man Suit
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for partial autonomy: AI products should act as an extension of the user, providing augmentation while keeping the human in the driver's seat.
mental_models.details: Instead of building fully autonomous robots that operate entirely on their own, we should build 'suits' that augment human capabilities (strength, tools, sensors, information) and allow the user to tune the level of autonomy.
mental_models.representative_quote: It's less Iron Man robots and more Iron Man suits that you want to build.

mental_models.label: AI Models as 'Ghosts'
mental_models.summary: AI models are ethereal, digital entities created by mimicking human data, not biological animals with hardware baked in by evolution.
mental_models.representative_quote: We're not building animals. We're building ghosts or spirits or whatever people want to call it, because we're not doing training by evolution. We're doing training by imitation of humans.

mental_models.label: Discriminator-Generator Gap
mental_models.summary: It is significantly easier for humans to evaluate (discriminate) the quality of a response than to create (generate) the ideal response from scratch.
mental_models.details: This explains why RLHF can improve models on creative tasks where writing the perfect answer is too hard for human labelers. Ranking five AI-generated jokes is much easier than writing the perfect joke.
mental_models.representative_quote: we basically train simulators of humans and RL with respect to those simulators

mental_models.label: Education as the Gym
mental_models.summary: In a post-AGI world, humans will pursue intense learning for cognitive flourishing and entertainment, much like people lift weights today despite having machines for manual labor.
mental_models.representative_quote: I often say that pre-AGI education is useful. Post-AGI education is fun. In a similar way, people go to the gym today.

mental_models.label: First-Order Approximations (The Spherical Cow)
mental_models.summary: Identifying the fundamental mechanism of a system and intentionally ignoring noise, edge cases, and efficiency details until the core is understood.
mental_models.details: For example, Micrograd is a 100-line Python script that serves as the first-order approximation of neural network training, showing backpropagation perfectly while ignoring tensors and memory movement.
mental_models.representative_quote: I'm trying to find, what is the thing that matters? What is the first-order component? How can I simplify it?

mental_models.label: Human-to-Agent Ratio
mental_models.summary: A lens for the future of digital work, analogous to the human-to-robot ratio in factory automation.
mental_models.details: Humans will transition from doing individual tasks to supervising multiple long-running AI agents, watching their work over minutes or hours and having them report progress back periodically.
mental_models.representative_quote: we are going to be talking about human to agent ratios where humans becomes a lot more supervisors of agent tasks in the digital domain

mental_models.label: LLM Training as Human Education
mental_models.summary: An analogy mapping the three major stages of LLM training to how humans learn in school.
mental_models.details: Pre-training is basic knowledge acquisition (reading the textbook's exposition). Supervised Fine-Tuning is imitating experts (studying worked examples). Reinforcement Learning is independent problem solving (doing practice problems to discover how to solve them).

mental_models.label: LLMs as Ghosts vs. Animals
mental_models.summary: Unlike animals, which are optimized via evolution and born with built-in hardware and instincts, LLMs are ethereal, fully digital entities trained by imitating human internet data.
mental_models.details: A newborn zebra can run immediately due to evolution encoding weights in its DNA. LLMs, lacking this biological optimization, must boot up their intelligence by observing algorithmic patterns in internet text.
mental_models.representative_quote: We're not building animals. We're building ghosts or spirits or whatever people want to call it, because we're not doing training by evolution. We're doing training by imitation of humans and the data that they've put on the Internet.

mental_models.label: LLMs as Savant Kids
mental_models.summary: Treating current LLMs as having the cognitive maturity of children, despite their vast memorized knowledge.
mental_models.details: Explains why LLMs cannot yet create 'culture' or operate as fully autonomous agents because they lack the necessary cognitive maturity across all checkboxes.
mental_models.representative_quote: I don't think they can create culture because they're still kids. They're savant kids.

mental_models.label: Lossy Compression of the Internet (The Zip File)
mental_models.summary: The parameters of a large language model act as a compressed, probabilistic representation of the internet's text, not an exact database.
mental_models.details: When you ask a base model for facts, it elicits a statistically probable list based on its compressed knowledge. The information is vague and probabilistic, not explicitly stored like a traditional database.

mental_models.label: Meat vs. Silicon Computer
mental_models.summary: Viewing human drivers as 'meat in the control loop' with high latency and limited awareness, compared to 'silicon computers' with 360-degree awareness and zero distractions.
mental_models.details: This model highlights the fundamental problem with human-driven transportation: humans are flawed 'meat computers' controlling high-kinetic-energy metallic objects.
mental_models.representative_quote: we have these metallic objects traveling incredibly quickly and with very high kinetic energy and we are putting meat in the control loop

mental_models.label: Sucking Supervision Through a Straw
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for the extreme inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning.
mental_models.details: A single final reward signal is used to evaluate an entire long, noisy trajectory of actions, blindly upweighting every single step taken in a successful path, even the mistakes.
mental_models.representative_quote: The way I like to put it is you're sucking supervision through a straw.

mental_models.label: Summoning Ghosts vs. Evolving Animals
mental_models.summary: A lens for understanding that LLM intelligence is fundamentally alien compared to human intelligence.
mental_models.details: Unlike human neural nets optimized for jungle survival (animals), LLMs are optimized for imitating text and solving math puzzles, resulting in alien entities (ghosts) that shouldn't be judged through an evolutionary lens.
mental_models.representative_quote: We're not "evolving/growing animals", we are "summoning ghosts".

mental_models.label: Swiss Cheese Model of LLM Capabilities
mental_models.summary: LLMs are incredibly capable across many disciplines but have random, unique 'holes' where they fail at seemingly simple tasks.
mental_models.details: An LLM might be able to solve complex Olympiad math questions but simultaneously fail to realize that 9.11 is smaller than 9.9 due to quirks in its training data.
mental_models.representative_quote: this is a hole in the Swiss cheese and there are many of them and you don't want to trip over them

mental_models.label: The Autonomy Slider
mental_models.summary: Technological progress is a continuum where humans progressively automate low-level tasks and raise themselves to higher layers of abstraction.
mental_models.details: Moving from writing assembly code to using C compilers, and now moving from writing manual code to supervising AI autocomplete and agents.
mental_models.representative_quote: I see AI as fundamentally an extension of computing in a pretty fundamental way.

mental_models.label: The Demo-to-Product Gap
mental_models.summary: The vast distance between a proof-of-concept that works in a controlled setting and a reliable product deployed in reality.
mental_models.details: Especially prevalent in domains where the cost of failure is high, like self-driving cars, which had perfect demos in 2014 but require decades of work to become scalable products.
mental_models.representative_quote: Whenever I see demos of anything, I'm extremely unimpressed by that.

mental_models.label: The Hacker's Perspective
mental_models.summary: Understanding complex mathematical or algorithmic concepts through the lens of code and physical intuitions rather than formal mathematics.
mental_models.representative_quote: ...explaining Neural Nets from "Hacker's perspective", relying more on code and physical intuitions than mathematics.

mental_models.label: The Lossy, Probabilistic Zip File
mental_models.summary: An LLM is essentially a compressed version of the internet stored within the parameters of a neural network.
mental_models.details: It is not an exact database, but rather a statistical recollection of information that remembers highly frequent data well and rare data vaguely. If you need fresh information, you must equip the 'zip file' with an internet search tool.
mental_models.representative_quote: Hi I'm Chach PT I am a one tab zip file my knowledge comes from the internet which I read in its entirety about six months ago and I only remember vaguely.

mental_models.label: The Verification Constraint
mental_models.summary: Autonomous AI improvement can only scale rapidly in domains where success or failure can be mathematically and objectively verified.
mental_models.details: An agent can rapidly optimize compiling a CUDA kernel because the reward is objective, whereas it wanders aimlessly if asked to generate a novel joke.
mental_models.representative_quote: Verifiable automated brute force search will consistently defeat legacy human intuition.

mental_models.label: Tokens, not Characters
mental_models.summary: LLMs do not see text the way human eyes do; they see chunks of text (tokens), which explains their failure at simple character-level tasks.
mental_models.details: When asked to print every third character of a word or count the 'r's in 'strawberry', the model fails because it only sees a few token IDs, not the individual letters.
mental_models.representative_quote: I really encourage you to think of these not as numbers but as unique IDs or like unique symbols so maybe it's a bit more maybe it's better to actually think of these to replace every one of these with a unique Emoji

mental_models.label: Universal Tokenization
mental_models.summary: The concept that a Transformer neural network does not inherently know the difference between text, audio, or images; it simply models statistical patterns of token streams.
mental_models.details: Different modalities are just different vocabularies (e.g., quantized image patches or audio spectrogram chunks) fed into the same architecture.
mental_models.representative_quote: the Transformer neural network itself it doesn't even know that some of the tokens happen to be text some of the tokens happen to be audio and some of them happen to be images it just models statistical patterns of token streams

heuristics.label: Build to Understand
heuristics.summary: If you can't build it from scratch, you don't truly understand it.
heuristics.details: Don't just read blog posts or copy-paste code. Build the repository, arrange the components, and get it to work to confront the micro-details you don't actually understand.

heuristics.label: AI Generates, Human Verifies
heuristics.summary: Design workflows where the AI does the heavy lifting of generation, and the human focuses purely on verification.

heuristics.label: Use GUIs for Faster Verification
heuristics.summary: Use visual interfaces (like code diffs) to speed up the human verification loop, because looking at visual information is faster than reading raw text.

heuristics.label: Agents for Boilerplate, Autocomplete for Novelty
heuristics.summary: Use LLM agents for boilerplate and familiar languages, but stick to autocomplete for novel, intellectually intense code.
heuristics.details: Models have cognitive deficits and over-rely on standard internet patterns. They bloat codebases and misunderstand custom implementations if given too much autonomy on novel tasks.
heuristics.representative_quote: I feel like the industry is making too big of a jump and is trying to pretend like this is amazing, and it's not. It's slop.

heuristics.label: Default to SFT Models
heuristics.summary: Default to standard SFT models for 80-90% of tasks; reserve 'thinking' models for hard problems.
heuristics.details: Standard models (like GPT-4o) are faster and sufficient for factual or simple queries. Reach for thinking models (like o1 or DeepSeek R1) only when facing difficult math or code problems where waiting is worth it.
heuristics.representative_quote: treat this as a as what it is which is a St stochastic system that is really magical but that you can't also fully trust and you want to use it as a tool

heuristics.label: Demo is works.any(), Product is works.all()
heuristics.summary: Recognize the massive gap between a working prototype and a reliable, production-ready product.
heuristics.representative_quote: Demo is works.any(), product is works.all()

heuristics.label: Dictate, Don't Type
heuristics.summary: Use voice input instead of typing whenever possible to reduce friction and speed up queries.
heuristics.representative_quote: don't type stuff out use voice it works quite well and I use this pervasively

heuristics.label: Discount Demos
heuristics.summary: Assume demos only represent the first 90% of the work; the remaining 10% will take 10x the effort.
heuristics.representative_quote: Whenever I see demos of anything, I'm extremely unimpressed by that.

heuristics.label: Double Down on the Dominant Sensor
heuristics.summary: When an inferior sensor (radar) and a superior sensor (vision) disagree, double down on the superior sensor rather than attempting sensor fusion.

heuristics.label: Escalate to Thinking Models
heuristics.summary: Try non-thinking models first because they are fast; switch to a thinking (reasoning) model only if the response fails on math, code, or logic.

heuristics.label: Force Step-by-Step Reasoning
heuristics.summary: Force the model to show its work step-by-step to spread out computation over multiple tokens.
heuristics.details: Because models have limited compute per token, asking them to create intermediate results allows them to 'think' and drastically improves performance on complex logic or math.
heuristics.representative_quote: models need tokens to think distribute your competition across many tokens ask models to create intermediate results

heuristics.label: Format Prompts as Web Pages for Base Models
heuristics.summary: To use a base model as an assistant, format your prompt to look like a web page containing a conversation.
heuristics.details: Base models are just document simulators. If you ask a question directly, they might just generate more questions. You must trick them into completing a document that looks like a helpful Q&A.
heuristics.representative_quote: it's not an assistant yet, it's a token autocomplete and second it is a stochastic system

heuristics.label: Hire for Agency
heuristics.summary: Hire for agency over intelligence, as intelligence is increasingly commoditized by AI.

heuristics.label: Hire for SWE First
heuristics.summary: Hire for strong software engineering first; teach deep learning on the job.
heuristics.representative_quote: Strong software engineering is the primary requirement. Except for the scientist role, deep learning interest or knowledge is only a bonus (we will teach you).

heuristics.label: Let Students Guess
heuristics.summary: Don't present the solution before giving the student a shot to guess it.

heuristics.label: Paste Reference Text
heuristics.summary: Paste reference text directly into the prompt rather than relying on the model's internal memory.
heuristics.details: The context window acts as working memory, giving the model direct access to the information, which produces significantly higher quality results than forcing it to rely on vague parameter recollection.
heuristics.representative_quote: when it's in the context window the model has direct access to it and can exactly it doesn't have to recall it it just has access to it

heuristics.label: Present Pain Before Solution
heuristics.summary: In teaching, always present the pain or problem before you present a solution.
heuristics.representative_quote: Present the pain before you present a solution.

heuristics.label: Rank by Quality x Obscurity
heuristics.summary: Rank recommendations by the product of their quality and their obscurity to highlight hidden gems.
heuristics.representative_quote: sorted by the product of (recommended * obscure), descending.

heuristics.label: Seek Entropy
heuristics.summary: Actively seek entropy in your life to avoid mental collapse and overfitting.
heuristics.representative_quote: You always have to seek entropy in your life.

heuristics.label: Step Outside the Loop
heuristics.summary: To achieve maximum leverage, the human must exist entirely outside the execution loop.
heuristics.representative_quote: If an AI has to wait for a human to review a result or prompt the next step, the systems velocity drops to zero.

heuristics.label: Use Code Interpreters for Math and Spelling
heuristics.summary: Always ask models to use a code interpreter for counting, spelling, or math tasks.
heuristics.details: Due to tokenization and limited compute per token, models are terrible at mental arithmetic and character-level manipulation. Lean on tools instead of their internal memory.
heuristics.representative_quote: we're using neural networks to do mental arithmetic kind of like you doing mental arithmetic in your brain it might just like screw up some of the intermediate results

heuristics.label: Verify and Own the Output
heuristics.summary: Use LLMs for inspiration and first drafts, but always verify their work and own the final product.
heuristics.details: Treat LLMs as extremely useful but fallible tools. They will hallucinate and have random capability gaps, so you must take ultimate responsibility for the final output.
heuristics.representative_quote: don't um treat these models as infallible models check their work use them as tools use them for inspiration use them for the first draft but uh work with them as tools and be ultimately respons responsible for the you know product of your work

heuristics.label: Wipe the Context Window Frequently
heuristics.summary: Always start a new chat whenever you are switching topics to clear the model's working memory and prevent distraction.

signature_quotes.label: Train Deep Neural Nets
signature_quotes.summary: His core professional tagline.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I like to train deep neural nets on large datasets 🧠🤖💥

signature_quotes.label: Building Ghosts, Not Animals
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing why he pushes back against the analogy of building AI like biological animals.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: We're not building animals. We're building ghosts or spirits or whatever people want to call it, because we're not doing training by evolution. We're doing training by imitation of humans and the data that they've put on the Internet.

signature_quotes.label: Agency > Intelligence
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting on what human traits are most valuable now that AI provides intelligence on demand.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Agency > Intelligence I had this intuitively wrong for decades, I think due to a pervasive cultural veneration of intelligence, various entertainment/media, obsession with IQ etc.

signature_quotes.label: Algorithm vs Efficiency
signature_quotes.summary: Advocating for minimal, dependency-free implementations.

signature_quotes.label: Anterograde Amnesia
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the limitations of LLM memory and learning post-training.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: LLMs are a bit like a coworker with Anterograde amnesia - they don't consolidate or build long-running knowledge or expertise once training is over and all they have is short-term memory (context window).

signature_quotes.label: Average Labeler Simulation
signature_quotes.summary: Demystifying the nature of conversational AI.

signature_quotes.label: Build to Understand
signature_quotes.summary: Advising on the best way to truly learn complex technical concepts.

signature_quotes.label: Demo vs. Product
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the significant gap between a working prototype and a reliable product.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Demo is works.any(), product is works.all()

signature_quotes.label: Education as Building Ramps
signature_quotes.summary: Describing his vision for Eureka Labs and how he views the challenge of teaching.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: In my mind, education is the very difficult technical process of building ramps to knowledge.

signature_quotes.label: English as Programming Language
signature_quotes.summary: Commenting on the shift towards natural language prompting for AI.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The hottest new programming language is English

signature_quotes.label: Hiring for SWE
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the hiring criteria for Deep Learning Engineers at Tesla.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Strong software engineering is the primary requirement. Except for the scientist role, deep learning interest or knowledge is only a bonus (we will teach you).

signature_quotes.label: Iron Man Suits
signature_quotes.summary: Advising developers to focus on partial autonomy and human augmentation rather than fully autonomous agents.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: It's less Iron Man robots and more Iron Man suits that you want to build.

signature_quotes.label: Job Reshaping
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the caveat of LLM-estimated AI exposure scores for occupations.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Many high-exposure jobs will be reshaped, not replaced.

signature_quotes.label: Meat in the Control Loop
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the fundamental problem with human-driven transportation.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: we have these metallic objects traveling incredibly quickly and with very high kinetic energy and we are putting meat in the control loop

signature_quotes.label: Parameters vs Context Window
signature_quotes.summary: Defining the cognitive architecture of LLMs.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: knowledge in the parameters of the neural network is a vague recollection the knowledge in the tokens that make up the context window is the working memory

signature_quotes.label: Post-AGI Education
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why people will still want to learn even after AI automates all cognitive labor.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I often say that pre-AGI education is useful. Post-AGI education is fun.

signature_quotes.label: RLHF is Gameable
signature_quotes.summary: Critiquing the limitations of RLHF compared to true RL.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I kind of see rhf as not real RL because the reward function is gameable

signature_quotes.label: Software 3.0
signature_quotes.summary: Referencing the shift toward Software 3.0 where prompts replace traditional code.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Software 3.0 is eating 1.0/2.0

signature_quotes.label: Summoning Ghosts
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the alien nature of LLM intelligence and how it differs from human evolution.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: We're not "evolving/growing animals", we are "summoning ghosts".

signature_quotes.label: The Human as Director
signature_quotes.summary: Defining the new strategic role of the software engineer in the age of AI agents.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The human is no longer the compiler, the optimizer, or the coder. The human is now the director of the token generating swarm.

signature_quotes.label: The March of Nines
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why reliable AI products take so long to develop after initial demos.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: It's a march of nines. Every single nine is a constant amount of work.

signature_quotes.label: The Zip File
signature_quotes.summary: Summarizing how users should conceptualize ChatGPT's knowledge and limitations.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Hi I'm Chach PT I am a one tab zip file my knowledge comes from the internet which I read in its entirety about six months ago and I only remember vaguely.

signature_quotes.label: Tokenization is at Fault
signature_quotes.summary: Identifying the root cause of many LLM quirks.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: ...we will see that a lot of weird behaviors and problems of LLMs actually trace back to tokenization.

signature_quotes.label: Tokens to Think
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the computational limits of a single forward pass.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: models need tokens to think

anti_patterns.label: AGI as a Sudden Jump
anti_patterns.summary: Believing AGI will be a sudden, magical 'God in a box' that instantly transforms the economy.
anti_patterns.details: Historically, transformative technologies diffuse slowly and face friction when integrating into society; they continue existing exponential trends rather than causing discrete vertical jumps.

anti_patterns.label: Assuming Linear Intelligence Scaling
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming LLM capabilities scale linearly like human intelligence.
anti_patterns.details: LLMs possess 'jagged intelligence'—they can solve complex math but fail at simple comparisons. Blind trust leads to catastrophic failures on seemingly easy tasks.

anti_patterns.label: Human-Only Documentation
anti_patterns.summary: Writing documentation and web interfaces exclusively for humans.
anti_patterns.details: AI agents are a new class of digital consumers. If documentation relies on visual layouts or instructions like 'click here', LLMs cannot natively parse or act on them, breaking agentic workflows.

anti_patterns.label: Jumping to Full Autonomy
anti_patterns.summary: Jumping straight to fully autonomous AI agents without human supervision.
anti_patterns.details: LLMs are fallible and hallucinate. If they generate massive outputs (like a 10,000-line code diff), the human becomes a bottleneck trying to verify it, defeating the purpose.

anti_patterns.label: Outcome-Based RL for Reasoning
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on outcome-based Reinforcement Learning for complex reasoning tasks.
anti_patterns.details: It assumes every step in a successful trajectory was correct, leading to high noise and inefficient learning from a single final reward ('sucking supervision through a straw').

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Automated Tools Without Understanding
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on automated tools (like PyTorch autograd) or copy-pasting code without building from scratch to understand the underlying math.
anti_patterns.details: It prevents the developer from building the strong intuitive understanding of gradient flow necessary to confidently debug and innovate on modern neural networks.

anti_patterns.label: Anthropomorphizing LLMs
anti_patterns.summary: Thinking of ChatGPT as a magical, sentient AI or assuming artificial neural networks function exactly like biological brains.
anti_patterns.details: Biological neurons are complex dynamical processes with memory, whereas artificial neurons in an LLM are extremely simple, stateless mathematical expressions that just transform inputs to outputs to simulate a human labeler.

anti_patterns.label: Complex Processing Pipelines
anti_patterns.summary: Designing complex, multi-staged processing pipelines with explicit algorithms instead of end-to-end networks.
anti_patterns.details: The model devolves into complex pipelines with many preprocessing stages, intermediate representations, and hyperparameters, often trained on distinct datasets with limited transferability.

anti_patterns.label: Endless Chat Threads
anti_patterns.summary: Keeping a single, endlessly long chat going for multiple unrelated topics.
anti_patterns.details: It fills up the context window with irrelevant tokens, which distracts the model, decreases its accuracy, and makes generating the next token slower and more expensive.

anti_patterns.label: Forcing Complex Logic in a Single Token
anti_patterns.summary: Forcing an LLM to answer a complex math or logic question immediately in a single token.
anti_patterns.details: There is a finite, roughly fixed amount of computation (layers) that happens per token. The model cannot squeeze arbitrary amounts of computation into a single forward pass.

anti_patterns.label: Human-in-the-Loop Execution
anti_patterns.summary: Keeping humans in the experimental execution loop for hyperparameter tuning or code generation.
anti_patterns.details: If an AI has to wait for a human to review a result or prompt the next step, the system's velocity drops to zero. Humans should only set constraints.

anti_patterns.label: Math-Only DL Education
anti_patterns.summary: Teaching deep learning exclusively through heavy mathematics.
anti_patterns.details: It creates a barrier to entry for capable software engineers who are interested in the field but lack formal mathematical backgrounds. Code and physical intuitions are often better.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Lidar and HD Maps
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on Lidar and High-Definition Maps for autonomous driving.
anti_patterns.details: It is unscalable to collect, build, and maintain high-definition maps for millions of customers globally. It requires expensive infrastructure to keep up to date.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on LLM Mental Arithmetic
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on an LLM's internal 'mental arithmetic' for character-level tasks like spelling or counting instead of using a code interpreter.
anti_patterns.details: Models have limited compute per token and do not see individual characters (they see tokens). Forcing them to calculate or count in a single forward pass often leads to errors.

anti_patterns.label: Requiring DL Expertise for SWEs
anti_patterns.summary: Requiring deep learning expertise for all deep learning engineering roles.
anti_patterns.details: It filters out exceptionally strong software engineers who possess the necessary foundational skills to build systems and can easily be taught the deep learning specifics on the job.

anti_patterns.label: Running RLHF Indefinitely
anti_patterns.summary: Running Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback indefinitely.
anti_patterns.details: The reward model is a lossy neural net simulation of humans. If optimized too long, the RL process will find adversarial examples that game the system and get perfect scores for nonsensical outputs.

anti_patterns.label: Treating Base Models as Assistants
anti_patterns.summary: Expecting a base model to act like an assistant and answer questions directly.
anti_patterns.details: Base models are just document simulators. If you ask a question, it might just generate more questions because it's acting like an autocomplete, not an assistant.

anti_patterns.label: Trusting AI Demos
anti_patterns.summary: Trusting AI demos as proof of imminent product readiness.
anti_patterns.details: Demos only represent the first 90% of reliability. The remaining 'nines' require massive, constant amounts of work, especially in domains where the cost of failure is high.

anti_patterns.label: Trusting Benchmarks for AGI
anti_patterns.summary: Trusting LLM benchmarks to measure progress toward AGI.
anti_patterns.details: Benchmarks are verifiable environments, making them susceptible to Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). Labs inevitably construct environments adjacent to benchmarks and 'grow jaggies' to cover them, crushing the benchmark without actually achieving general intelligence.

anti_patterns.label: Trusting LLM Math Blindly
anti_patterns.summary: Trusting LLM math calculations or data analysis without tool use (Python interpreter) or code review.
anti_patterns.details: Models without access to a Python interpreter will try to do complex math in their 'head' and confidently hallucinate incorrect results. Even with tools, they might make sneaky implicit assumptions in the code.

anti_patterns.label: Venerating Intelligence Over Agency
anti_patterns.summary: Venerating intelligence and obsessing over IQ when evaluating or hiring people.
anti_patterns.details: Intelligence is becoming widely available ('on tap') via AI, making it less of a differentiator. Focusing on intelligence causes you to miss out on agency, which is much more powerful and scarce.

anti_patterns.label: Vibe Coding Novel Systems
anti_patterns.summary: Using autonomous LLM agents ('vibe coding') to write novel, intellectually intense code rather than just boilerplate.
anti_patterns.details: Models have cognitive deficits and over-rely on standard internet patterns. They bloat the codebase, misunderstand custom implementations, and try to force standard production boilerplate where it isn't needed.
